sentences.text: Rena Zabielak.
My name is Rena Zabielak, Z-A-B-I-E-L-A-K.
Litwak.
L-I-T-W-A-K.
The 23rd of March, 1924.
7-- nearly 72.
I was born in Warsaw, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in, in Poland.
Yes.
I was born in Warsaw, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the 23rd of March, 1924.
My mother's name was Mindla, Mindla Litwak [NON-ENGLISH] Poncz, P-O-N-C-Z.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was the first child to my dear parents.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was three, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had an addition of the family, my only brother, Abraham, Adek.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were looked after by my mother on the beginning.
And then we had a governess, which used to look after us.
The governess spoke French.
And she taught us speak-- she spoke to us in French.
When I was six, I was sent to a private school in Warsaw, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was quite a way from home.
But my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mother used to take us to school or our governess-- to take me to school.
Because my brother started a few years later.
My father had import and export of fruit on [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a place where all the fruit trade was in Poland.
Yes, my family were-- my grandfather used to be an antique dealer.
But the grandfather from my father's side was a teacher a [NON-ENGLISH].
He used to teach children in Hebrew.
And he had-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH].
My, my maternal grandparents live not far from us in Warsaw.
But my paternal grandparents lived over the river.
Vistula went through Warsaw.
And they used to live in Praga.
And how far away was that?
It wasn't very far.
But it took about a half an hour to get there, because Warsaw wasn't a big city.
By tram.
Or sometimes they used to take a [NON-ENGLISH] to go there.
Later, we used to take a taxi.
My mother didn't work.
But my mother used to help other people, poor people.
She done a lot of charity work.
And the same my father.
No.
He went-- I went to a school for girls.
And my brother went for boys.
And in his school was the teacher of Polish was Professor Palapan.
He was very famous.
And together with him in the class was a boy, the famous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Count-- Jewish Count-- Kosovitski.
He was a friend of my brothers.
Very happy.
And we had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was about 30 girls in my class.
And on of-- you see, I don't remember very well all my friends.
Because it is a long time ago.
But one which I remember very well was Mita Kaszizner, who was the daughter of one of the Bundists.
And then later, I found her, after the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had Marisha Meisel.
She was a daughter-- actually, a granddaughter of that famous Rabbi Meisel's.
But I can't say that I remember a lot.
Because it was quite a long time ago.
And a lot passed by in this, those days.
Quite religious.
My mother was more religious than my father.
Yes.
We kept Shabbat.
And we-- family always used to meet on Fridays and Saturdays.
With the grandparents.
Sometimes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Depends what it was.
A matter of fact, I remember my grandfather, my paternal grandfather, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was very ill.
He had pneumonia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother cooked a special fish for him.
And I went there on Saturday, by tram.
When I arrived, my grandfather said to me, how did you come?
I said, I walked.
Then he said it is a much greater sin to say a lie than to travel on Shabbos.
And I've got to admit, ever since, I am not lying.
I try not to lie.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because he was a very, very fantastic man.
Both my grandfathers were very nice.
But funny enough, I lost them both in six months.
Yes.
One died in '37.
It was like October, November.
He lived with us for a few years, because Grandma died.
And the other one died on the beginning of '38.
No.
But my grandmother did.
One grandmother died when I was a very little girl.
Yes.
I was seven years old.
I remember, because every year in the summer, we used to go on holiday.
In those days, you used to go on holiday for two months.
And we went to a place called Druskieniki.
And my mother were sitting by the river Nemunas.
And something bit her in the neck.
And she was very, very ill.
And they rang up that Daddy should come.
And that time my grandmother died.
And my mother didn't know till we came back to Warsaw.
Her mother.
And when she came back, she was sitting Shiva.
Because first they took my mother to Warsaw for an operation.
And then she came back to Druskieniki to be with us.
But we had a maid with us there.
And we stayed with the maid, my brother and I.
I tell you, I remember exactly.
Because you see, we children in Warsaw, you didn't in Poland, before the war, you had to buy books.
And all the books were in the hands of Jewish people.
And it was a street called [NON-ENGLISH].
There were the shops, which they used to sell books.
When you went to buy books, the, the [NON-ENGLISH] were standing outside the shops and calling, don't buy from a Jew.
And then you felt very rotten when as a child, you went to buy a book, and they told you don't go into a Jew to buy a book, when you were Jewish.
And funny enough, I had a friend called Zabche Yonash.
And her father had a shop with books.
And I always-- my mother used to take me there or my governess, Eva.
And when we came, they were standing outside the shop.
And they said, don't go in.
This is a Jewish shop.
When I was a little bit older, you know, the Poles celebrate the 3rd of May.
And it is a big, you know, it is a holiday [BACKGROUND NOISE] for liberation of Poland, a national holiday.
And they always march the army, you know, everybody.
And on the end, march the students.
And I had a friend what her auntie had a flat on the place where they marched through.
And when I went there, and we were standing and say no-- and on the end, when they said, the Jews have to leave Poland.
The Jews have to go to Palestine.
And can you imagine how we felt?
We were standing there on the balcony, watching them.
And that's what they said.
Then since my-- So how were you at that point?
About 11, 12.
Then sometimes-- and after this incident, we hardly went there.
We didn't want to watch it.
Because we were scared that will hit us.
Because the Poles were not very nice people to us.
And then my father, in '37, he was an importer of fruit and exporter.
And he went to England, because he was exporting onions to England.
And he had a friend who lived in Berlin-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was in '37, '38.
And he met him at the station in Berlin.
And when he found out what was going on there, he was very, very upset.
And he came and he said, oh, I hope that this won't start in Poland, the atrocities, which they are doing in Germany.
And I-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
Yeah.
Atrocities.
Germany.
And he hoped that it won't start the same in Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You see, every year in the summer, we used to go for two months holiday.
Then we went first to Druskieniki.
Then we were-- and then we went, in 1935, '36, we went to a place called Sopot.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a [NON-ENGLISH], Danzig, what it is now Gdansk.
And there was a most beautiful place called Sopot.
And I used to go there.
And we had a lovely time, every year.
Children, we enjoyed very much.
No, he used to come to us for a few-- always for a few days.
And we had a holiday.
It wasn't that we went-- like here, you go to a hotel and you stay in a hotel.
We used to hire a flat.
And we stayed in the flat.
And we used to go out for dinners.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a lovely-- and there we used to go to the opera, to the opera in the forest.
And Mommy used to take us every day to concerts, because she liked music.
And it was-- we had a very nice time.
Yes.
Classical music.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But all the good things come to an end.
And on the 1st of September, 1939, the war broke out.
And it was a very hard time.
Warsaw was bombed and burned.
Yes.
And funny enough that I had an aunt, and she had a flat on the ground floor.
And I felt only safe there, behind the cup-- cupboard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wardrobe, on the bed, lying down.
When the bombs were flying, I thought there they would never reach me.
But on the end of September, the Germans walked into Warsaw.
And the trouble started from the beginning.
I was 15.
First of all, we couldn't go to school.
We-- but our teachers organized that we used to have lessons, every time in a different place, about six, eight, or sometimes 10 girls, and, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and my brother as well, you know.
Our governess, we didn't have anymore.
I think she stopped coming to us when I was about 12, 13, being with us.
She was Jewish.
She was Jewish.
And then when-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then as soon as the Germans walked into Warsaw, it was a lot of trouble.
They used to catch people in the street.
And then they used to come to-- if they didn't catch enough people for work, they used to come to the houses to take them out.
And funny enough, we had a very lovely flat.
And the doors in the flat were painted the same as the paper was on the wall.
And I let in the German through the kitchen.
And I let out my father with the front door.
And the Germans were looking, and they didn't find my father.
And when they left with the kitchen door, I let my father in.
Through the front door, because he was just-- then we pretended that he got typhus.
And when say heard that somebody got typhus, [NON-ENGLISH] fever, they run away.
No, he still had a business.
But sometimes he was at home.
And they wanted to-- they were catching for work.
And he didn't go to the shop on the day or something.
And that's what we used-- it was very hard.
He had some people working for him.
They were Jewish as well.
They were, they were also.
Jewish.
And also what it was, as soon as they walk in, they start to come in to the houses of rich people, take out the furniture and so on.
And one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they came to us.
And we-- and funny enough, the same day, my uncle came in.
And he said you've got to make a note with those furniture.
Because if the Germans will come, they will take all the furniture away.
Then they start dismantling the furniture, putting from one place to the other and so on.
When the Germans walked in, they looked at the ceiling.
And there was the most beautiful lamp.
They pull it off.
Then we found out that my mother had diamonds hidden in that lamp, but nobody could say anything.
They took it away.
They took away the typewriter.
They took away canteens of cutlery and so on.
But you couldn't do nothing about it.
And also there were Polish women, which knew rich people.
And they used to, when they wanted a nice fur coat or something, then they called the Germans.
The Germans went in and took out from the Jewish houses fur coats, clothes, whatever those Polish women wanted.
They used to come, and they used to collect.
But that was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was Hanukkah 1939, '40.
And the Germans gave an order that every Jew has to wear an armband with a-- it was a white armband, about 10 centimeters wide with a star of David.
And then say could recognize every Jew.
And--
How did you feel about that?
Very bad.
Because when you went out, people looked at you.
They knew straight away that you are Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

And they also form-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not a committee, a Jewish, like we've got here, the [? boat ?] of [? guardian. ?] It was a Jewish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] place, where they used to look after the Jews, which were in Poland.
And the chairman of that committee, the president, was a Mr.
Chernyakow.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And in 19-- for new year, the Germans gave an order-- they were building a wall.
Nobody knew what it is all about.
But then they said they are going to make a ghetto.
No, I didn't.
And then they built the wall around Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said everybody has to come and to live in that part of Warsaw.
Funny enough--
Well-- our flat was in that--
Yes, compound.
And my father's family lived in Praga.
And as it was a large flat, they all moved in to us.
It was very happy, because the whole family was together.
But--
Yes, my two grand-- my grandmother was with us.
But at that time-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] at that time, when everybody moved into that place, the compound, the Germans closed the ghetto.
And nobody could get out.
And then you felt like closed in, in a--
--Box, prison.
And the trouble started.
And people didn't have enough food.
They were dying from starvation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But when it started, actually, my father's business was outside the ghetto.
And all the merchandise was there.
And my father was very depressed.
And he knew a Polish policeman.
And this policeman said, I will take you out from the ghetto.
And you'll [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bring it in, this stuff, to the ghetto.
